data.title: The Prospects For NANO Nuclear Energy
data.description:
Crude oil, natural gas, coal, and biofuels are the traditional energy commodities. Nuclear energy has had its problems over the years, notably, Chernobyl and the Japanese Fukushima Daiichi accident caused many countries to abandon nuclear power solutions. However, in 2025, nuclear energy is back in vogue as the demand for power will surge over the coming years.

NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. (NASDAQ:NNE) shares have soared over the past few months, with prospects indicating even higher prices. NNE’s company profile states:

The company’s website highlights its role in the nuclear sector and its plans to become “the only vertically integrated nuclear fuel business of its kind in North America.” At $46.63 per share on October 10, NNE shares could have significant upside potential.

The demand for power is rising by leaps and bounds

Power, or electricity generation, can come from various sources. Crude oil, natural gas, and coal are traditional energy commodities that utilities use to generate electricity. While the United States employs a mix of energy sources, including natural gas, renewables, and coal, China and India depend heavily on coal.

Meanwhile, power demand is rising, with projected electricity demand expected to grow by 25% by 2030 and 78% by 2050 from 2023 levels. The U.S. power grid faces challenges in 2025 and will struggle to keep pace with demand. Increasing demand from electric vehicles, the growing number of data centers, and technological advances have put a tremendous strain on the U.S. power grid, making nuclear power generation a rising energy source for the future. While nuclear power has risks, it is a cleaner solution when compared to traditional fossil fuel inputs.

A bullish trend since the April 2025 low

At $49.18 per share, NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. had $1.97 billion in assets under management.

The daily year-to-date chart highlights NNE’s over 250% rally from the April 21 low of $17.26 to the October 7 high of $60.51 per share. On October 7, NNE reached a record high. While NNE shares pulled back to below $50 on October 10, the path of least resistance remains higher.

NNE is a liquid stock in a booming sector

With its market cap just below the $2 billion level, NNE shares trade an average of nearly 4.9 million shares daily, making it a highly liquid stock.

According to the World Nuclear Association, the United States is the world’s largest producer of nuclear power, producing approximately 30% of worldwide nuclear electricity.

In May 2025, President Donald Trump signed a series of executive orders titled “Reinvigorating the Nuclear Industrial Base, Reforming Nuclear Reactor Testing at the Department of Energy, and Ordering the Reform of the Nuclear Regulatory Commission.” The EO’s goal is to reestablish the United States as the global leader in nuclear energy by increasing capacity from 100 GWe to 400 GWe by 2050. The EO’s shorter-term goal orders the Department of Energy to prioritize work “with the nuclear energy industry to facilitate five gigawatts of power uprates to existing nuclear reactors and have ten new large reactors with complete designs under construction by 2030.” The executive orders provide financial incentives for the nuclear power industry.

The nuclear power sector is experiencing a boom, driven by the surge in AI data centers, growth in domestic manufacturing under the Trump administration, the continued electrification of vehicles and buildings, and the expansion of the oil and gas sector. One of the growing asset classes, cryptocurrencies, is a technological financial advancement. Crypto mining and blockchain technology increase electricity demand. All these factors put strains on the U.S. electricity grid, and nuclear power is a cleaner and efficient solution for the coming years.

Meanwhile, technology companies are investing billions in AI data centers, which only escalates power demand.

Mostly attractive Seeking Alpha Factor Grades - The reason to ignore profitability

NANO Nuclear Energy’s bullish path reflects the increasing demand for a U.S. nuclear power-generated grid solution.

Seeking Alpha’s Factor Grades for NNE award the highest A+ in growth and an A in the stock’s momentum, given the price action since the April 21, 2025, low. The D in revisions and failing grades in valuation and profitability reflect that NNE has posted losses over the past quarters since the stock began trading in May 2024.

There are many success stories of companies that posted losses while their shares rose before ultimately turning a profit.

An M&A candidate with a market capitalization below $2 billion

The critical factor supporting NNE and other companies in the nuclear power sector is the overwhelming power demand from leading technology companies, which are making substantial investments in artificial intelligence. AI demand from data centers will put a massive strain on the U.S. energy grid, and cash-rich tech companies could address their future energy requirements through equity investments or even acquisitions of utilities. At just under a $2 billion valuation, NNE could be an attractive takeover candidate for the nine companies with over $1 trillion valuations.

While nuclear energy solutions are likely to support energy companies, M&A activity from the technology sector could create a bidding war for companies like NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. I rate NNE a buy during the current dip that has taken the shares below the $50 per share level.

data.title: Baker Hughes: A Bet On Data Centers, Energy Infrastructure, And LNG
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Baker Hughes Company (NASDAQ:BKR) is one of the Big Three oilfield services companies, playing a vital role in the oil, gas and energy industries and going through significant changes and improvements over the past years, backed by solid macro developments and internal changes.

Right now, the market seems to be pricing in quite a lot of potential ahead, thanks to the industry’s tailwinds, backed by future AI and LNG demand, while the current environment could contradict these expectations and result in weaker spending on their customers’ side, potentially resulting in worse-than-expected results from BKR, so let’s take a deeper look.

The company delivered a very impressive Adjusted EBITDA margin improvement over the past half a decade, but I believe their ability to keep delivering that remains questionable. I say that because we have to keep in mind what happened during this time. This has been achieved through an amazing upcycle for oil and significant disruptions following the pandemic and the war in Ukraine.

Firstly, very importantly, following the war, the US began taking advantage of EU sanctioning and slowly cutting their dependency on Russia’s LNG. Even before Trump’s US-EU $750 billion LNG deal, the US was very quick to capitalize on this, with a very significant drop in the EU’s imports of Russian gas and a 119% jump in LNG imports from the US in 2022 compared to 2021. This fell by ~19% in 2023 and continued to grow in 2024, with the exports of US LNG to Europe (6.3 Bcf/d) still significantly higher than in 2021 (2.4 Bcf/d).

To this, add the fact that the very significant jump in oil prices refueled the industry’s investing activity, with record levels of CAPEX (and cash flows) reported by most oil and gas producers globally. This resulted in more investment activity, meaning a higher need for drilling, monitoring, well construction, and everything else that BKR does, which in turn allowed the company to invest in itself and develop alongside the industry.

Sure, their own actions also played an important role, we can’t deny that. BKR went through some significant portfolio optimizations over time, specializing even more in a rapidly developing environment and expanding their offerings into higher-margin areas (e.g., going for lower-carbon solutions over certain lower-margin oilfield solutions), with a massive recent acquisition that I’ll mention below being a good example of their portfolio rebalancing.

Still, we see a relatively weak first half in terms of cash flow from operating activities and free cash flow, although the second half tends to be the better one in terms of cash generation. Still, they expect the second half of the year to be better:

Following a strong first half and a positive outlook for the second half awards, we are confident in achieving IET's full year order guidance range of $12.5 billion to $14.5 billion. Looking beyond this year, we see continued momentum for power solutions, sustained growth in new energy, and a robust pipeline of LNG and gas infrastructure opportunities, all of which support a constructive outlook for orders.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a very good position, with the current assets nearly covering the company’s current liabilities plus long-term debt. However, this will change significantly following a recent acquisition that I’ll mention in more detail below, although it will still remain solid for a company in its industry, and they plan to reduce their leverage significantly after the deal.

Their Q3 report is set to be released on October 23, and although I generally don’t give that much importance to a single quarter, what they’ll say in this one should be very important. We’ll get a potential update about the current environment and how they see the remainder of the year (with potential hints into how they see 2026), as well as details about the acquisition and other portfolio adjustments, on top of potential insights about industry tailwinds that could be useful for many other investments, so it’s definitely something worth paying a lot of attention to.

Dividends & Buybacks

BKR is paying a small ~1.9% dividend on top of ~1.6% in buybacks, which is significantly lagging behind most of the oil and gas or adjacent industries. As they say during the Q2 Earnings Report:

Our balance sheet remains in a very strong position. We ended the quarter with cash of $3.1 billion and net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 0.6x and liquidity of $6.1 billion. We also returned $423 million to shareholders. This included $227 million of dividends and $196 million in share repurchases. We remain committed to returning 60% to 80% of free cash flow to shareholders.

This is ultimately the result of the valuation, as even if they had a 100% payout ratio, the combined yield couldn’t sustainably be more than something below 5%, which is simply not good. This doesn’t seem like the type of investment to get for dividends and buybacks today, but rather for strategic importance and growth potential, in my opinion.

Risks & Opportunities

Rate cuts are probably the main near-term opportunity for BKR and their clients. Following the cut in September and with two more expected this year, we might be looking at significantly better costs of debt that could allow both the company and their clients to invest and develop once again. However, there’s also the risk of seeing rate cuts combined with tariffs and other issues lead to hard times for the US and global oil and gas trade, so this is a double-sided factor to keep an eye on.

They talked about the impact of tariffs, estimating a $100 million to $200 million hit to their consolidated EBITDA this year—assuming no further tariffs or retaliation, saying the following during their Q2 report:

So as we take all of these different variables and recognizing their dynamic, we have confidence in our mitigation actions that we put in place immediately. And we have, as you know, a flexible global supply chain. And so we maintain our previously communicated estimate of the $100 million to $200 million net EBITDA impact for the year. But just as a reminder, this does not -- this assumes the recently announced tariffs are implemented as scheduled, but it does not assume any further trade policy escalation, including retaliatory tariffs.

The company kept announcing various projects in recent months, such as supplying the liquefaction equipment for Sempra (SRE)’s Port Arthur LNG Phase 2, supplying up to 50 subsea trees for Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (PBR) Brazil oil fields (plus a multi-year agreement with them), joining a partnership to develop one of the world’s largest geothermal projects, and many more.

They also announced the $13.6 billion acquisition of Chart Industries, Inc. (GTLS), whose shareholders recently approved it. The company generated about $4.2 billion in revenue and $1 billion in adjusted EBITDA in 2024, with 65 manufacturing locations globally, with BKR adding that this acquisition will help it expand its exposure to high-growth markets such as LNG, data centers and clean energy. This is a massive deal, and combined with the company’s recent developments mentioned above, it’s likely a reason why the stock performed well even in a weakening environment. Based on their estimations, BKR sees run-rate synergies growing from $95 million in year one to $230 million in year two and $325 million in year three thanks to SG&A optimizations, supply chain efficiencies and facility optimization, while commercial opportunities add further upside potential.

This highlights something very important about BKR: the company does much more than just help the oil industry. On top of the oilfield drilling, completions, etc., they also have significant exposure to natural gas, LNG, and energy, especially following this recent acquisition.

As I said before, I remain very bullish on LNG, and we’re currently looking at potential to see a “Third Wave” of LNG, with projects such as Golden Pass LNG, Plaquemines LNG (Phase 1 & 2), Corpus Christi LNG Stage III, Port Arthur LNG or Rio Grande LNG (Trains 1-3) more—potentially adding a lot more supply (potential for over 200 MTPA in new capacity).

This can then result in even more infrastructure needed, both for construction (which Baker Hughes already benefits from) and from the environment it would then create, so there’s definitely potential for BKR to see more growth ahead.

Plus, data centers, cloud and all the other tech that we’re referring to now will require massive amounts of energy and I remain bullish on that overall, and Baker Hughes has potential to develop from that significantly, especially if we see a rise in data centers near oilfields, such as the one that LandBridge Company LLC (LB) and NRG Energy, Inc. (NRG) are following a strategic collaboration to explore the development of a potential data center site in Texas' Delaware Basin. This could potentially offer a significant opportunity for BKR, although it’s still in a very early stage of development/emergence.

They actually see this as part of their “Horizon Two” market landscape, marked by gas, decarbonization and AI-led power demand surges. After that (2029 and beyond), they see growth coming from industrial and new energy markets, on top of the solid fundamental growth in the industry.

But still, oil prices remain very important for BKR at the end of the day. Yes, they aren’t a producer, but oil is falling to a point where oil and gas companies become forced to cut some CAPEX, and more pressure on its price for longer can result in less activity, fewer new projects, less drilling, etc., meaning a clear and direct hit to BKR’s revenues. Especially before the Chart acquisition, BKR had about 15% of its revenue coming from other industries than oil and gas, getting to ~30% following the deal. So, they are developing significantly beyond O&G, and oil itself is now taking a smaller and smaller stake in the company’s revenue mix.

What it means is BKR will also depend on things like OPEC’s decisions (recently announced another production hike for November) and political uncertainty—just see how they crashed ~20% in a couple of days back in April following Trump’s tariffs. However, as I mentioned in the TotalEnergies SE (TTE) analysis, the company projects that global oil supply (demand too) is expected to fall quite a bit in the medium term, especially now when virtually every oil producer is entering into a CAPEX-cutting territory due to the falling oil prices, which of course has an effect on the future supply, while other macro developments could continue to solidify the demand.

Regarding OPEC, they also commented during the latest earnings, saying that:

As we look into the second half of the year, we expect continued volatility as OPEC+ accelerates the return of its 2.2 million barrels per day of idle production into what we anticipate will be a soft market. Ultimately, until all excess OPEC+ barrels are absorbed by the market, we anticipate oil-related upstream spending will remain subdued.

As for BKR, they might display kind of a delay when it comes to weakness in oil, as projects aren’t cut overnight, so I believe it can take quite a bit until BKR would show it. This could also explain the current stock’s performance, other than LNG, and especially data centers potentially bringing some more demand for the stock, which might unfortunately fade quickly if they start reporting issues.

Still, if BKR can use this valuation to keep developing by acquiring peers that come at better valuations, this doesn’t have to be a bad thing. However, since Chart was an all-cash transaction despite the company’s very strong stock price, I highly question the management’s ability to take advantage of it. The acquisition was okay and makes sense from a strategic perspective, but using cash and significantly affecting the balance sheet is very questionable, although they say net leverage is projected to be 2.25x at close and plan to deleverage to 1.0-1.5x within 24 months.

So, a questionable move (buying with cash/debt over stock) but it doesn’t mean they’re bad. If we look at the bigger picture, we have a company that’s very much ready to take advantage of future trends, and with the surge in AI-related energy demand and beyond it, we’re likely looking at a lot of growth ahead for the industry.

Peer Comparison

Compared to the rest of the Big Three—SLB N.V. (SLB) and Halliburton Company (HAL)—as well as smaller and more specialized players like Weatherford International plc (WFRD), Tenaris S.A. (TS) and TechnipFMC plc (FTI), we see once again what I said throughout the analysis: a relatively high valuation compared to peers from most points of view.

Oddly enough, despite their focus on improving it, we see the weakest EBITDA margin (not adjusted) in the group and overall average profitability margins. Again, this valuation could be a tool that would allow them to buy said competitors and deliver significant synergies and per-share increases, assuming they want to issue stock to get them.

Valuation

For this valuation, I want to use the combined company from the beginning since the deal is very likely to go through. Starting with BKR’s last year’s free cash flow of $2.25 billion and Chart’s ~$390 million, we get to $2.64 billion in FCF in 2024. I’ll go for a 5% CAGR in the first 5 years, as well as $50 million in synergies per year (below their estimates since we can’t be sure how much is added to the cash flow, for a better margin of safety) for the first 3 years thanks to rate cuts and other industry tailwinds.

As a note, the company’s FCF/share grew at a CAGR of about 3.1% from 2015 to 2024—yes, only that from an industry slump into a good year, but let’s say they have significantly improved since 2020 on a fundamental basis.

After that, I’ll assume a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.75% terminal growth rate, plus a 8.5% WACC (assumptions: ~9.5% cost of equity, ~3.5% cost of debt), an estimated net debt following the acquisition of Chart of about $15.8 billion, as well as 985 million shares outstanding. This way, the Enterprise Value is $53.20 billion, and after deducting net debt, the equity value is $37.40 billion, leading to an intrinsic value per share of $37.97, slightly below the current level. And keep in mind that this doesn’t consider a potential downturn in case the environment results in less demand for their services.

Conclusions

Overall, I rate BKR a Hold now due to the little margin of safety and unattractive valuation, combined with the risk-reward it comes with at these prices. The company is fundamentally solid and should improve in the future, but the macro headwinds and internal risks remain, even if the industry has great long-term tailwinds and the company is adjusting itself to be able to capitalize on them better.

data.title: WTI Crude Steadies Near $61 As Traders Await Breakout From Tightening Triangle
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

​WTI crude oil is trading around $61.3, consolidating near its critical support zone after a sharp pullback earlier this week. Price action has narrowed into a descending triangle structure, with a horizontal base around $61 and a series of lower highs pressing against declining resistance.

This tightening formation signals that a decisive breakout is approaching, leaving traders on edge as fresh catalysts loom.

Technical setup signals mounting pressure

From a technical standpoint, the $61 region remains a key inflection point. A clear break below this level would expose the next supports at $60 and $58.5, potentially extending the recent downtrend and confirming renewed seller dominance. On the upside, a rebound through $62.5–$63.1, where the 50- and 200-period EMAs converge, would mark the first sign of recovery. If momentum builds beyond this zone, targets around $65 and $67 could come back into play, levels that have repeatedly capped rallies since mid-September.

Momentum readings reflect this equilibrium between opposing forces. The RSI sits near 41, suggesting muted bullish drive as buyers struggle to regain control. Price continues to hover just below short-term EMAs, underlining the need for a firm defense of the $61 floor to prevent the structure from tilting decisively bearish.

Geopolitics and supply trends shape sentiment

Fundamental factors remain mixed. The recent progress in Israel-Hamas ceasefire negotiations has reduced the geopolitical risk premium that previously buoyed prices, triggering short-term selling. However, new U.S. sanctions targeting over 50 Iranian entities and vessels involved in energy trade have reintroduced supply-side uncertainty. Meanwhile, OPEC+’s restrained production hike indicates that producers remain cautious amid fragile demand forecasts.

According to EIA data, U.S. crude inventories rose for a second consecutive week, suggesting softer refinery demand. Yet, inventories at Cushing and across refined products declined, keeping overall market balances tight. This push-and-pull dynamic between geopolitical risk and inventory shifts continues to anchor oil within its current range, with traders awaiting the next major catalyst to define direction.

Outlook

Overall, WTI crude sits at a crucial juncture. A clean break below $61 would likely accelerate bearish momentum toward $58.5, while a successful defense could pave the way for another rebound toward $63–$65. The interplay between technical compression and evolving fundamentals—particularly developments in Middle East diplomacy, U.S. sanctions enforcement, and inventory trends—will likely dictate near-term volatility.

In earlier coverage, the $61–$61.5 region was highlighted as a key zone where short-term buyers had repeatedly defended against deeper losses. That support remains vital in determining whether oil can sustain its broader consolidation or extend into a more prolonged correction.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

data.title: Ørsted: The ADR Is Going Up Significantly, And Here's Why
data.description:
Over the past few weeks, the ADR ticker for Ørsted A/S (OTCPK:DNNGY) has gone "gangbusters". By this, I mean that we've seen a very significant improvement in share price and returns. Usually, this would mean that I would take a "victory lap", and if you look at the way the stock has performed since my last article, I think you'd agree that a 6-7x outperformance of the SPY is actually a very good performance. You can see the outperformance from my last article below.

And if interested, you can find that article here.

However, note that the article title is not that I'm taking a victory lap. Nor do I think it would be suitable here. The reason is quite simple. The return or the change in share price we see in the ADR DNNGY is not reflected in the native ticker, ORSTED on the Copenhagen stock market. In the past month, as we can see on the ticker page on Seeking Alpha, DNNGY is up over 128%.

That is obviously impressive. However, the native ticker is not up even close to as much. In fact, the native ticker is down, from around 200 DKK to 122 DKK, and while we're up as of the time of writing this article, we're not seeing the sort of reaction for the native ticker that we're seeing for the ADR.

In this article, I will show you why the company seems to be recovering and also see why there is such a difference in native and ADR performance here.

Also, see what the company might be delivering in the next 1-3 years, and update my thesis, last covered in June of this year.

So let's see what we have here.

Ørsted—Upside is difficult to see, even with the recent trends considered.

So, a few things have happened since my last article. I won't go through the company's entire "fall from grace" in this piece. That has been extensively covered in the linked article above and others. Instead, we'll focus on relatively recent trends.

Despite everything that's happened, Ørsted is still a leader in wind power. With close to 10 gigawatts of gross offshore capacity, of which over 5 GW is net capacity as of year-end of the last complete fiscal, Ørsted is a leader in this. With over 80% of the company's EBITDA coming from these offshore farms, the company's business mix is unique, even amongst renewable players in Europe, which tend to be more diversified.

The issue is that much of Ørsted's previous upside was based on very generous subsidies from the European and other governments. Based on an average lifespan, I'll argue that these offshore wind farms that Ørsted has actually do not offer a very attractive lifespan—about 7 years for the ones the company has.

Subsidies have gone down. Not just in the USA, where the company in various projects is bearing the brunt of headwinds, but also in Europe. The company's entire strategy was based on, because of lower subsidies in Europe, expanding to the US with an ambitious 5GW in various offshore wind projects. At this point, we know how that went—it did not go well.

Between project halts, inability to find investors for projects like Sunrise Wind (Ørsted blamed hostile policies from US government, making investors wary of offshore wind), restructuring, massive strategic shifts, and ongoing legal action (never a risk neutral, regardless of how good your case is), it would be kind at this point to call the company's thesis "complex".

The reason the ADR is up, as I see it, is that investors are over-interpreting (or focusing) on company efficiencies, such as the recent news that the company is cutting 1/4 of its workforce, as well as raising $10B (close to) in a recent share sale. The fact that the Danish state actually subscribed to a full 50.1% of this issue should not be a surprise if you follow the trends and news. Like Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), Ørsted is a bit of a "darling" for the Danish state—it's not going to abandon it.

That does not make the company a good investment, however.

The capital will be used to cover financing for the above-mentioned Sunrise, strengthening the capital structure. I believe this, together with the company's efficiency measures, as well as the rumors of a 50% sale of its UK-based offshore project, is responsible for the trends.

From a net perspective, we can now clearly state that the company is shoring up its balance sheet and significantly improving its fundamentals. However, it's doing so through dilution of original shareholders—and if the company were to sell part of its Hornsea 3 project, it's likely not at the price that investors expected the project to generate a few years back.

In fact, let me remind you that 2025 has not been a good year for Ørsted. Over 12B DKK of impairments booked in US projects alone so far, with further reductions to its net investment, tells a very clear story of the renewable market and the state of investments today—namely, that they are having trouble finding interested parties, further confirmed by the trends in Sunrise Wind.

It's a good idea when you're looking at risks for a company and a company doing a rights issue to see at what discount that company is doing the issue. When I first saw the issue, I did put some extra sugar in my coffee.

Why?

Because the 900M+ shares with dilution, with a share price of 66.6 DKK implied an over 65% discount to the latest closing share price.

Now, it turned out oversubscribed (another reason for the upside, I believe), but I believe this speaks volumes of the appeal, or rather lack of it, in this sector at this time. I made it very clear in my June article that Ørsted, while having some incredible "safeties" for the long term, comes at a very high risk factor. If you decided to swallow that risk/reward ratio and invest in DNNGY for an 80%+ outperformance of the SPY, then the only thing to do is to congratulate you on your superb short-term return.

However, I believe two things are crucial to understand at this point. First, Ørsted's earnings, no matter how much they're trying to dial down and fix the US, are tied to the US offshore. And because of President Donald Trump's and his administration's stance against wind, I would take fifteen steps back from any project in this sector unless I got it at "pennies on the dollar". I did expect the stop order against Revolution Wind in Connecticut to be lifted, as it was at the end of September of this year. But this is only one example, and more might be coming. I expect stop orders against projects like Sunrise. I do not doubt that A) Ørsted will beat them/have them lifted eventually, and B) The company now has the balance sheet to weather these orders.

But they will be materially impactful and dilutive on earnings—that much is clear to me.

Ørsted's positives can be stated with relative confidence—and I maintain that they are true. First, the rights issue is necessary and positive. While dilutive and at a discount, there really was no option. I like how oversubscribed it was as well. Secondly, interest rates continue to fall, and as a renewable player, Ørsted is favorably exposed to this. Thirdly, outside the US, the company's UK operations are doing well (a light point, actually), and once the indexation clauses (inflation-related) come into effect and remaining earnings stabilize, Ørsted could see some advantages.

But, as we'll see in risks later, there are a number of reasons right now why I believe that if you hold the ADR, you should consider rotating and taking home that profit.

Valuation and lack of upside for Ørsted going forward necessitate a cut in recommendation. I say "Hold" or rotate.

DNNGY is a 0.33x ADR: That means that the current value of the ADR, because it's a 0.33x ADR, implies that native Ørsted should trade at 468 DKK, based on current FX.

Does it?

No, Ørsted is trading at 122 DKK.

Less than a month ago, DNNGY was trading at less than half of the current value, and it would imply closer to 192 DKK, which by the way was also the level that Ørsted's native share traded at. It seems to me that the ADR has not been corrected for the rights issue, or there are other things at play here.

Whatever the case, however, the difference between DNNGY and Ørsted does not make sense at this time. I would also point out things like the recent S&P Global downgrade to BBB- for the company.

When it comes to the ADR, I only have one thing to say: get out. While this isn't a recommendation (I don't give stock "recommendations" as such), it's a very strong stance, which I will argue for in my risks and thesis below. If I owned a single share of the ADR, I would sell it.

The Ørsted native ticker trades at 17.8x P/E. The DNNGY ticker trades at almost 40x P/E. Native Ørsted can, at the current share price, be argued based on a 16-17% EPS growth annually over the next few years, to have an annualized P/Side of 13.3%. No dividend remains for the company at this time.

There is a case to be made for investing in Ørsted—at some point. I just don't believe that point to be now. The ADR DNNGY has gone through a spike in share price that I cannot say is "fair" or likely to be maintained, given the company's current trends. The native ticker Ørsted is fraught with uncertainty following a 115% dilution in shares after a massive rights issue that saw the share price dive from close to 200 DKK to below 120 DKK.

The main argument I make is that there are far better investment alternatives out there in the utility sector. Compared to another company with massive renewables, I would say SSE plc (OTCPK:SSEZY), which I invest in and which I believe has a significant upside.

If you wanted to go for Ørsted, I would put the company's target below 200 DKK. I do this cut from 300 DKK due to a 115% dilutive rights issue—and I could actually go lower and still be "in line" with my expected multiples. The discount was far, far above what I expected—my estimate was 30%. This was more than double that. I do not believe Ørsted has any moat left, and I expect a 2025E EBITDA of 23B DKK or thereabout, below the 24-27B DKK guidance. The UK will carry the growth, but I believe the USA will be more of a drag than the market expects.

I hold a negative view of Revolution and Sunrise in the US, which is why I cut growth estimates to 10-12% as opposed to 15-20% market expectations—and I forecast that Ørsted will need to continue to invest 150-190B DKK between the period of 2025-2029—and this includes both Sunrise Wind as well as the potential impacts of tariffs. Remember, this is not done either.

For that reason, I consider a fair value above 300 DKK to be speculative and a PT above 200 DKK to be the same. Even now, despite the fact that I technically have the company rated a "Buy" for the native, the covered ticker DNNGY is not a "Buy", because the current share price, coupled with the ADR multiple, puts it at a hold. A 200 DKK share price comes to a $10.5/share PT for DNNGY, and this makes it a "Hold".

Risks & Considerations

Risks to this company are, lacking a better word, significant. The company's rights issue, while absolutely necessary, is significantly dilutive. It has been described by some as a dilution simply to keep Sunrise Wind alive—and while not incorrect, it's also necessary. Still, it's worth noting that this is what the company has done. Just how dilutive was it?

The Number of shares for Ørsted increased by 115% based on 900M shares, which represents an EPS dilution of over 60%. Also, there are continued very significant political risks in the US, which is the company's by far most important geography. Even now that the order against Revolution Wind is lifted, the next risk is an order against Sunrise Wind.

And while interest rates are pointing down, there are plenty of indicators that they will not go down further. Any increase in overall interest would be a net negative to this company.

Thesis

Ørsted is now a heavily speculative renewables company that remains one of the relevant market leaders in the entire segment. It's state-owned, has a well-filled pipeline, and is one of the most conservative financing pipelines and maturities when it comes to utilities. But it's also exposed to some major market risks in the US, has had issues with its US-based projects, and recently completed a massively dilutive rights issue.

The ADR DNNGY has spiked to more than 2x the level it should be based on the multiple for the ADR, which is why I view this ADR as a rotation target with a "Hold" rating.

The upside is highly dependent on how the market for the offshore market develops and how the 2025-2029E period works out—I don't see a clear upside given multiple recent failures for the company, but the current price dictates this can be a "Buy". However, I would be looking at alternatives prior to investing here—and I say again, the "Buy" is for the native, not the ADR.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a price target of 200 DKK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of October 2025 for the native but a "Hold" for the ADR due to the discrepancies described herein.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Walmart: An Actual Defensive Buy During Weak Economic Conditions
data.description:
Last time I covered Walmart Inc. (NYSE:WMT), I rated it a Buy thanks to its strong e-commerce growth and its ability to relocate supply chains and pass on tariff-driven cost increases. This time, I’m reiterating that Buy rating for a different reason. With the economy showing signs of weakness, Walmart stands out as one of the most appealing defensive plays in the market.

Investment thesis

The economy is weakening, and historical Fed rate cuts have preceded major market downturns. Walmart Inc. (WMT), as a defensive stock, has historically performed well in recession-driven crashes, outperforming the S&P 500 in every instance. Despite an expensive valuation, with the company trading at almost double the sector median EV/EBITDA (20.6 vs 11.1), investors may continue to invest in WMT due to its large reach, solid operations, and great performance during weak economic conditions, pushing the stock price even higher until the economy signals a recovery.

Market Conditions

In the US labor market, private businesses cut 32,000 jobs in September instead of the forecasted 50,000 gain. Also, Non-Farm Payrolls increased by only 22,000 in the latest reading, continuing a weakening labor market trend that is moving closer to a phase of broader job cuts across the US economy. The manufacturing sector has also been in contraction territory since November 2022, but what has sustained growth in the economy is the services sector. The Institute for Supply Management’s Services PMI, an important leading indicator, was mostly above the 50 mark, which represents growth in the services sector. However, after falling from 52, the last reading of 50 was also accompanied by weak internal data. The new orders index in the services PMI, which tends to be a leading indicator of the services PMI itself, fell from 56 to 50.4 in the last reading. Inventories started to contract, and employment remained in contraction territory. There was a positive increase in the backlog of orders, from 40.4 to 47.3, but it is still in contraction territory. Additionally, consumer confidence is near the lowest readings of all time, even below previous market crashes, which is negative for the economy.

Interest Rate Cuts Correlation With Market Crashes

Given the weakening economy, the Federal Reserve acted by cutting interest rates, but if we look at past rate cuts, there is a strong correlation with recessions, which could imply that the interest rate cuts happen in a delayed manner, not avoiding the recession. During these periods, the S&P 500 has had its biggest downturns. The most large recent interest rate cuts were in 1990, 2001, 2008, and 2020.

In 1990, the Fed was already cutting interest rates, but the economy still entered a recession, and later the Gulf War shocked the market alongside a sharp drop in consumer confidence, which led to the S&P 500 falling around 20%. In 2000, the dot-com bubble burst, with many unprofitable tech companies collapsing and the S&P 500 losing about 50% of its value in two negative years. In 2008, the housing bubble burst, and the S&P 500 fell approximately 57% between the end of 2007 and the beginning of 2009, with the Fed cutting rates near zero and launching Quantitative Easing to boost liquidity in the market. In 2020, the economy shut down due to the pandemic lockdowns, and the S&P lost about 30% of its value in 3 months.

Walmart's Performance During Market Crashes

Periods like these bring the investors’ attention to less cyclical stocks, like consumer staples, healthcare, and utilities. So, given the disconnect between the weakening economy and the strong performance of the S&P 500, along with the sharp drop in new orders of the services PMI index, which has been the last pocket of growth in the economy, I started revisiting consumer defensive stocks, focusing on their performance during past interest rate cut cycles and market crashes, and what I saw made me rethink my portfolio.

Note that I’ll use two distinct periods for comparison: the entire phase when interest rates are falling and the specific window when the S&P 500 is declining. Typically, rate cuts begin first, then the S&P crashes and starts recovering within the interest rate cut period.

So, during these last four market crashes, the 1990s, 2000s, 2008s, and 2020s. WMT consistently delivered positive results.

In the 1990 interest cuts, during the whole interest cut period, WMT returned 109%, and during the market crash itself, WMT returned negative 3%, above the market's negative 10% return.

In the 2000 interest rate cut period, WMT returned 7%, and in the market crash of 2000, which lasted almost three years, WMT returned 8% while the S&P 500 dropped negative 41%.

In the 2007 interest rate cuts, WMT returned 8%, and during the market crash, 9%, while the S&P 500 returned negative 46% during the crash.

In 2019, WMT returned 12% during the interest rate cuts and 6% during the market crash, while the S&P returned negative 10% during the crash.

These results highlight the consistency of this stock in the weakest market conditions, but what really caught me by surprise was that other stocks in the sector don’t perform as consistently as WMT. After gathering all consumer staples stocks, I filtered for only positive returns during the interest cut periods starting in 2000 (because many of the stocks were not trading before 2000). Out of 78 stocks that had price history going back that far, only four had positive returns during the interest cut cycle, which is marked by strong economic downturns. Of these four, WMT seems to be the most resilient, and Cal-Maine Foods, Inc. (CALM) closely follows. The other stocks have one or two good years, then a year of losses, sometimes bigger than the market's.

WMT Strengths

Looking at WMT's results, revenue is growing faster than the sector median, and the bottom line is also improving. In 2021, WMT's net margin was 2.37% and in the last reading, net margin (TTM) increased to 3.08%, which reflects the company's improvements and adaptability to new market conditions. On the balance sheet, Walmart has around $62 billion of debt with $9.4 billion in cash to help balance it out. The interest rate cuts will help the company to refinance its debt and reduce the $2.3 billion paid in interest each year, which may improve net margins, and boost earnings. With WMT's EBITDA of $42.8 billion, the company's debt is not a burden.

Not only that, but Walmart’s international expansion and growing e-commerce segment are strong drivers for the company. WMT has become a digital ecosystem that has scale, resilience, and is adapted to new consumer preferences. Its global presence diversifies revenue sources, while its digital investments improve customer reach. Additionally, Walmart’s massive scale and bargaining power allow it to pass costs to consumers without significantly affecting demand, which reinforces its resilience during periods of economic weakness.

WMT has strong profitability, momentum, and growth. Even though valuation metrics are distant from the sector median, with price to earnings at 40.6 vs. the sector median of 16.2 and EV/EBITDA of 20.6 versus a sector median of 11.1, I believe that the profitability, growth, and size of the company are far more important than valuations at this moment. In this kind of economy, investors flock to the biggest, most stable companies, willing to pay a premium for safety, and WMT’s strong track record during these periods makes it even more appealing. So, I am positive that prices may continue to increase, pushing valuations even higher, given a continuation of the weak economic conditions. As for a target price, my last article’s DCF model pointed to $123.07. But my real focus isn’t a specific price; instead, it’s monitoring key leading indicators like manufacturing and services PMIs and consumer confidence for signs of recovery. If the economy shows a sustained recovery, I plan to rotate my portfolio toward higher-risk assets. Additionally, to be clear, I’m not looking at WMT as a long-term investment but rather as a hedge in the event of a broad market downturn that could last a few months or years.

Risks

A key risk of investing now in WMT is that it depends on the market viewing the stock as a defensive play, which has happened, but it may not happen again. The sector also depends on continued economic weakness, that is, if your goal is to outperform the market. If the economy picks up again, with strong consumer and business confidence, investors may shift focus to riskier assets, which may cause WMT to lose value.

The sector is highly competitive. WMT faces competition from many players. To stay competitive, WMT needs to keep investing in technology, logistics, and giving promotions, which can reduce the company’s margins.

Conclusion

Given the current economic conditions, with a weakening labor market, slowing manufacturing and services sectors, and historically low consumer confidence, I believe increasing exposure to WMT is a wise move. I used to think that any “good” stock within the consumer staples sector could serve as protection against a market crash. However, after analyzing past periods of similar weakness, I realized that despite the overall strength of staples during downturns, investors clearly favor size and stability in the largest player of the sector. Additionally, Walmart’s international expansion, strong e-commerce growth, and massive scale allow it to maintain pricing power and pass higher costs to consumers, making it resilient in these economic conditions. To me, the stock is a Buy.

data.title: Homebuilders Struggle
data.description:
The foundation for homebuilder stocks has cracked over the last few weeks. As shown in the chart of the S&P Homebuilders ETF (NYSEARCA:XHB), an attempt to re-take the 50-DMA at the start of the week failed miserably, and the ETF enters today down 6.5% since Monday's open.

What had been a solid uptrend off the April lows has now broken for XHB. (You can take a closer look at the chart for XHB here.)

All but two homebuilder stocks in our snapshot below (from our Trend Analyzer tool) are down over the last week, with most down 5% or more. The recent move lower has left most of them in oversold territory, with three at "extreme" levels (>2 st. dev. below 50-DMA).

NVR (NVR), LGI Homes (LGIH), and TRI Pointe (TPH) are the three most oversold builders relative to their normal trading ranges, while KB Home (KBH) and D.R. Horton (DHI) are the next-most oversold.

The only housing-related stock in our snapshot that's trading above its 50-DMA is TopBuild (BLD), which has bucked the trend and traded up 8% over the last week. The reason? Two days ago, TopBuild announced it was purchasing Specialty Products and Insulation - a wholesale distributor of insulation products - in an all-cash deal for $1 billion. While oftentimes companies doing the acquiring in an M&A transaction will initially see their shares trade lower, investors liked what they saw from this TopBuild purchase.

Original Post

data.title: Shutdown Threatens Next Week's Inflation Report, Making Fed's Job Harder
data.description:
By James Picerno

The government’s September report on consumer price inflation (CPI) is scheduled for release next week (Wed., Oct. 15), but the main uncertainty isn’t about the numbers. Rather, the question is whether the data will be published on time. At the moment, the odds don’t look encouraging, thanks to the ongoing government shutdown.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics is reportedly working on a plan to bring some employees back from furlough to publish the data, but as of this writing there’s no official confirmation.

This much is clear: Incoming CPI updates are especially critical for the Federal Reserve these days as it attempts to navigate between signs of a slowing labor market and a modest but persistent rise in the inflation trend in recent months.

Whether there’s new inflation data or not, the next monetary policy meeting is approaching, set for Oct. 29. Even with a normal schedule of inflation updates, the Fed’s job has become increasingly challenging as it tries to decide if tariffs will lift inflation. As a result, the central bank must decide if inflation risk should take a back seat to prioritizing the downshift in hiring lately.

Rate cuts are still the expected policy path

Most Fed policymakers support further rate cuts, according to the minutes from the last FOMC meeting on Sep. 16-17, when the central bank announced a ¼-point cut – the first reduction this year.

TMC Research’s Fed funds model continues to indicate that policy remains moderately tight, which implies that there’s still a case for rate cuts while maintaining a relatively hawkish bias to combat inflation. Our current estimate: the effective Fed funds rate is a bit more than one percentage point above the neutral rate, based on modeling inflation, economic activity and other macro factors – a spread that’s remained relatively stable in recent weeks.

The market continues to expect another rate cut at the end of the month, based on the Fed funds futures estimate, which is pricing in a 95% probability that the central bank will ease policy for a second time this year on Oct. 29.

No sign of inflation anxiety in the 10-year yield

Concern about future inflation appears muted in the bond market via the US 10-year Treasury yield. The benchmark rate has been trading around 4.1% lately, which is near the lower end of its range so far in 2025.

Mixed outlook for inflation from various sources

The jury’s still out on whether tariffs will raise inflation in the months ahead. The recent pickup in the 1-year change for headline and core CPI through August suggests that pricing pressure may be strengthening. The core reading rose to a 3.1% increase vs. the year-ago level, the highest since February and well above the Fed’s 2% target. Headline CPI’s rise is softer, but it’s also turned up in recent months.

Despite moderately hotter inflation, Fed officials generally see the bigger risk in a slowing job market, which aligns with easing policy to stimulate hiring.

New York Fed President John Williams supports more rate cuts even as inflation has edged higher. “The risks of a further slowdown in the labor market is something I’m very focused on,” he said on Thursday. He reasoned that inflation risk has eased in recent months compared with the initial view after the increase in tariffs in the spring. Williams also expected that the tariff-related impact on inflation to fade over time.

Yet there are hints that inflation could rise further before peaking. The New York Fed this week updated its monthly consumer survey, noting: “Median inflation expectations in September increased at the one-year-ahead horizon to 3.4% from 3.2% and at the five-year-ahead horizon to 3.0% from 2.9%.”

At the betting market Kalshi, a slight majority (52%) expect that headline CPI will top 3.0% in September (up from 2.9% in August).

If the shutdown continues into the second half of the month, the CPI report for September might be scrapped entirely and raise questions about whether October data will be published.

“If we’re up to October 22, October 23? That — to me — is almost a cutoff point where we should start to think [that] we’re probably not going to have an October CPI,” said Omair Sharif, the president of Inflation Insights, a consultancy.

The Fed, of course, remains open, thanks to funding that’s independent from Congress, and so the central bank is scheduled to make a decision on monetary policy at the end of the month, with or without updated inflation data from the government. It was a tough call before the shutdown, and it’s not getting easier if key data updates are postponed or cancelled. The risk of a policy mistake, as a result, could be rising.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: BAE Systems: Potential For Further Upside
data.description:
BAE Systems plc (OTCPK:BAESY) is one of the many aerospace and defense stocks that have had a bullish momentum since the start of the year. The stock is up 89%, outpacing most of its listed peers based in the U.S.

This makes BAESY appear highly overvalued, especially given the budget-related reservations from the EU members to raise their defense spending. Despite the collective efforts of European nations being channelized through the Rearm Europe 2030 initiative presented earlier this year, the U.S. is expected to spend more than the combined EU spend. Naturally, investors should be inclined more towards defense contractors in the U.S. who haven’t rallied as much as their European counterparts. But there is an opportunity with stocks like BAESY if the comparison is restricted to European companies.

As can be seen, the sharp rise in BAESY shares does not align with other European defense giants such as Rheinmetall AG (OTCPK:RNMBY) and Saab AB (publ) (OTCPK:SAABY). On a YTD return basis, the stock is lagging its European peers in large multiples, with SAABY and RNMBY delivering 1.97x and 2.82x higher returns so far, respectively. Taking this perspective, one could possibly change their view on BAESY and see an opportunity for further gains. In this thesis, I present my bullish stance on BAESY, backing it with favorable prospects within the European defense landscape.

Business Segments

Based out of London, BAESY delivers tech-enabled systems and services covering land, sea, air, cyber, and space. The business operates through five segments: Electronic Systems, Platforms and Services, Air, Maritime, and Cyber and Intelligence. Solutions offered by BAESY have commercial, military, intelligence, and law enforcement applications.

The Electronic Systems segment delivers electronic warfare, navigation and surveillance systems, next-gen military communication capabilities, flight controls, sensors, and electric propulsion systems. Through this segment, BAESY also complements its space operations, including space electronics and relevant mission-enabling technologies.

Platforms and Services is a growing segment that primarily builds weapons and munitions and combat vehicles. With major operations stretched across the United States, the UK, and Sweden, the segment also offers management of munition facilities along with repair services for the U.S. Navy.

Next is the Air segment that is aligned with the Future Combat Air Systems (FCAS) program, holding contracts for major projects across the globe. A key component of this segment is FalconWorks research and technology development center which facilitates futuristic technologies for air combat. Besides that, this segment is also involved in building Tempest, which is a sixth-generation fighter jet expected to replace Eurofighter Typhoon by 2035.

The Maritime segment involves maritime and land operations within the UK. This segment is also capturing business opportunities within international markets such as Australia and Canada. BAE Systems is delivering design consultancy & support services on the Canadian River Class destroyer program and is also involved in the $5.1 billion Hobart Class combat systems upgrade program.

Finally, the smallest segment in terms of revenues is Cyber & Intelligence. It encompasses digital intelligence and security solutions for government enterprises in the U.S. and the UK.

As can be seen, the company’s £75 billion funded demand backlog is being led by the Air segment, which accounts for 35% of the total. This is followed by a Maritime backlog of £21.7 billion, and the combined total for these two segments makes up 64% of the current backlog.

European Defense

BAE Systems could be a critical player within the European Union’s defense spending plans for the next decades. This could be marked with some exciting opportunities, not just for the company’s existing capabilities, but also for the new ones. Back in March 2025, the European Commission proposed the ReArm Europe Plan 2030, which would involve extending more than €800 billion in defense-related spending across the region. This announcement came after the Commission had issued a report, European Defense Readiness 2030, that focused on the region’s preparedness for military threats, especially from Russia. Some critical areas highlighted were missile defense capabilities, artillery ammunition, and drones and counter-drone technologies. All these resonate with BAESY in the context of their offerings.

With the region’s security heavily dependent on the NATO alliance, the NATO summit back in June called for the EU members to raise their budgetary allocations towards defense. The response was encouraging as 23 EU members within the NATO alliance agreed on spending at least 3.5% of their respective GDPs on defense by 2035. They further committed to spend an additional 1.5% on defense-related infrastructure, which brings the total to 5% of GDP. However, many members face financing concerns as they do not have the requisite fiscal flexibility to extend such resources. And this is where the Commission’s Security Action For Europe (SAFE) plan comes in, which involves €150 billion in low-cost loans to be disbursed within member states to back their spending targets. Another important component is the injection of private capital through support from European Investment Banks.

The figure above indicates the necessity to allocate bigger chunks for different European nations in proportion to the threat they face from Russia. Poland, for instance, has expressed concerns on that front after recent drone strikes by Russia and was allocated almost one-third of the total SAFE Plan budget, i.e. €43.7 billion.

Exploring Space

BAESY acquired Ball Aerospace from Ball Corporation for $5.5 billion back in February 2024. Through this acquisition, the company established its Space and Mission Systems (SMS) business to augment its Electronic Systems segment. The SMS business deploys numerous capabilities through cutting-edge programs, including National Defense Space, Data Analysis and Cyber, Tactical Missions, Spacecraft, and Satellite Ground Systems. The establishment of the SMS business has resulted in close to double-digit expansion of segment revenues during the first half of 2025. Through its Missile Warning and Missile Tracking (MWMT) capabilities, the company is playing an integral role within the U.S. Golden Dome Missile Shield project. The company received a $1.2 billion contract from the U.S. Space Force to build 10 satellites that enable missile detection. The company also provides space surveillance and situational awareness capabilities to the U.S. Space Force through its SBSS Block 10 space vehicle.

On the commercial side, business offers Satellite Communications through Electronically Steerable Antenna (ESA) software that links mobile platforms to different orbits. Other commercial offerings include Satellite Rideshare “Space as a Service”, Cyber A2 Attack Alert, and Azalea Radio Frequency Mapping.

The above chart is the breakdown of $4.67 billion in revenues generated from the Electronics Systems business. The impact of SMS operations has been significant, contributing 21% to the segment sales, which is almost 7% of the overall company sales. Going forward, BAESY should try to expand this segment further with many opportunities arising within the global space landscape.

Earlier this year, McKinsey shared their decade-long projections for the space segment, which will offer $1.8 trillion opportunity for global economic growth by 2035. Besides that, the Golden Dome project will lead to significant opportunities for players like BAESY, as the Congressional Budget Office estimated around $831 billion post-initiation spend on this project in the next two decades. The company has a chance to build upon their ongoing engagements with the Space Force to capture more business.

Futuristic Air Combat

BAESY is also capitalizing on the Global Combat Air Program (GCAP), a joint venture between the governments of the UK, Japan, and Italy to develop a sixth-generation stealth fighter jet i.e., Tempest. Since its inception in 2022, this program has paved the way for collective efforts between public and private entities aimed at developing advanced air combat capabilities. At the end of 2024, BAE Systems established a joint venture company, Edgewing, in collaboration with Japan Aircraft Industrial Enhancement Co., Ltd. (not publicly traded) and Italian MNC Leonardo S.p.a.(OTCPK:FINMY). This collaboration marks a major milestone within the broader GCAP program.

In July 2025, as part of the GCAP program, BAESY announced that they had initiated the manufacturing process for The Demonstrator, which is a piloted supersonic aircraft. This aircraft will facilitate the testing and integration of advanced stealth-compatible features that are to be deployed within Tempest. The aircraft is expected to be ready in the next three years and is being developed in collaboration with Rolls-Royce Holdings plc (OTCPK:RYCEY), MBDA UK Limited (privately held), and the UK Ministry of Defense.

Within the Air segment, another area that requires special attention is BAESY’s drone and counter-drone capabilities. The company is heavily focusing on Uncrewed Air Systems (UAS) through its FalconWorks research and technology development center. In fact, drone volumes were amongst the primary drivers of 9% growth in the Air segment sales during the first half of 2025. In my view, the company still has a lot of room to capitalize on this market, which is expected to reach around €40 billion by 2034.

In September, the company announced its partnership with Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) to design uncrewed aerial drone systems. Both companies will join hands to develop advanced electronic warfare and attack systems, along with upgrades to the existing crewed systems. Moreover, in 2024, Chief Executive Charles Woodburn also reiterated the company’s stance on expanding within this space. He signaled towards BAESY’s inorganic growth strategy, where they would aim to acquire drone manufacturers and designers to benefit from the global defense boom.

Platforms and Services Growth

Order intake for this segment continued to be strong during the first half of 2025. With a segment order backlog of $19.5 billion by the end of the first half, this segment is exhibiting impressive growth through rising production of ground combat vehicles. This resulted from a strong demand for the U.S. Army’s Armored Multi-Purpose Vehicles (AMPV), which are toppling the previously used M113 and are undergoing full-rate production, leading to margin expansions.

Similarly, Combat Vehicle 90 (CV90), which is manufactured by BAE Systems Hagglunds, is also going through upgrades for integration of advanced weapon systems and is garnering international demand. During the last week of September, the company secured a $396 million contract modification from the U.S. Army for manufacturing A4 vehicles based on Bradley Fighting Vehicle capability. This capability is critical for the Military’s Armored Brigade Combat Team (ABCT). Just a couple of weeks prior to that, BAESY also secured another contract worth $440 million for more than 200 additional A4 Bradley Fighting Vehicles.

The management had indicated earlier this year that they are preparing to ramp up their annual capital investments beyond the current £1 billion level. In my view, the Platforms and Service segment will see a significant chunk of these investments, as the segment offerings carry strong relevance to Europe’s ongoing super-cycle of defense spending.

Financials and Valuations

BAESY’s second quarter results show sales of £14.6 billion during the first half of 2025, reflecting a 9% topline growth. A resilient operational performance led to a marginal expansion of EBIT margin to 10.6% for the first half compared with 10.4% in the same period last year. The company registered an EPS of £0.323, which grew 3% relative to the EPS during the first half of 2024.

The biggest growth in EBIT came from the Platforms and Services segment, which increased by 37%, followed by Electronic Systems at 17%. In absolute terms, Electronic Systems generated the highest EBIT, followed by the Air segment, as these two accounted for 67% of the total EBIT during the period. The management also revised its 2025 earnings guidance upward, anticipating total revenue growth of 8% to 10%, similar to the expected growth in EPS. Although the first half resulted in negative free cash flows of £368 million, management has offered an optimistic 3-year guidance where FCF will exceed £5.5 billion by 2027.

In my view, the free cash flow targets seem unrealistic given the company’s commitment towards large capital expenditures in the coming years. However, cash acquisitions in recent times, along with a few more in the coming years, will generate decent cash returns, allowing the company to maintain a strong free cash flow position. This will also let the company continue on its 24-year run of semi-annual dividend payments.

The company recently announced its final semi-annual payout of $0.7231 for 2025. This gives a monumental dividend growth of 17% for 2025. This indicates management’s confidence in the future business pipeline, growth across all segments, and a strong cash conversion ratio. The resulting yield of 1.7% is higher than the sector median of 1.46% which should attract investor interest.

The company’s valuation metrics do not inspire confidence, as it ranks low compared to the sector median on all parameters. However, the sector median does not include too many companies that are operating predominantly in Europe. As indicated at the start of the article, if one strictly looks at the European peers, the story turns different.

To further substantiate the analysis, I compare BAESY along several valuation metrics with 3 major European peers, i.e., Rheinmetall AG, Saab AB, and Rolls-Royce Holdings. Although there are clear distinctions within this peer group, on many fronts, they possess similarities that make them comparable for valuation purposes. Rheinmetall has relevance to BAESY as both companies deliver combat vehicles and systems, military munitions, and cybersecurity and surveillance services. With a global reach, both companies overlap on government clientele, including the United States, the UK, Mainland Europe, and the Middle East.

Saab also has relevance with Rheinmetall in terms of advanced combat jets, security technology, communication devices, and integrated helmet systems. The two companies have also collaborated on the design and development of the Gripen fighter jet. Both serve common customers, having major government contracts with the UK and Sweden.

Finally, Rolls-Royce is also a major industrial player in the global aerospace and defense market, based in the UK. It is similar to BAESY in terms of generating significant business from the United States and the UK. Power systems for civil aviation and marine operations are one area where the two companies overlap.

I used the trading comparables methodology to make a comparison across 5 valuation multiples on a trailing twelve-month basis. It is understood that these companies vary in terms of their respective size, and hence, I added rigor to my analysis by using market capitalization-weighted averages for all multiples. The table above clearly shows that BAESY is significantly undervalued compared to these European peers across all metrics. My workings show the average discount for all metrics used in the analysis turns out to be 51%. Based on that, my price target for BAESY is $162.43.

Risks

There is a perception around BAESY to be overvalued, and this is one of the major risk factors in the short run. Many investors will be reassessing their positions and might end up liquidating their holdings in the stock. This would lead to the emergence of a bearish momentum, which might get fueled if there is a large block of institutional sales. Hence, investors should keep an eye on any short-term movements and book entry points at lower levels.

The management’s earnings presentation for the second quarter shows BAESY carrying a net debt of £5.6 billion by the end of June 2025. It is understood that a large part of this debt had been raised in the previous 3-4 years to support the company’s growth initiatives. However, one cannot ignore the related interest expense on a continuing basis. In the last few years, from 2022 to 2024, the company incurred a total interest expense of £1.16 billion. Although the future cash flow generation ability of BAESY cannot be underestimated, the debt servicing costs do create a hurdle and would affect the profitability. Investors should monitor the company’s future capital allocation plans, which require significant outlays. Financing the majority of those expenditures through borrowings could pose a significant risk for long-term value creation for shareholders.

Conclusion

Looking at BAESY’s growth in new orders and demand backlogs across all segments, I am very confident about medium- to long-term prospects. The company could benefit from Europe’s focus on strengthening its defense base and curbing reliance on American defense contractors. Despite risks of short-term movements in the share price amid profit taking by investors, I see strong upside potential. This optimism is also because of the stock’s underperformance relative to European peers, as discussed in the article. My stance is a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Job Worries Sour Sentiment
data.description:
By James Knightley

Confidence remains on its lows

The October preliminary reading for the University of Michigan consumer sentiment index has changed little from the final September print - slipping to 55.0 from 55.1, but at least it is above the 54.0 consensus prediction. The breakdown shows current conditions rising 0.6 points to 61.0, while the expectations component dropped 0.5 points to 51.2. To give you some context, the average consumer confidence print over the past 25 years was 91.8, so the responses are rather gloomy. Moreover, we are at the same sorts of levels as during the GFC in 2008 and when inflation rose above 9% in 2022.

The details show the reason for such pessimism is worries about jobs, with 63% of households expecting unemployment to rise over the next 12 months. There are also weak readings for perceptions of household finances and comfort in retirement. The perceptions of "is government doing a good job fighting inflation and unemployment?" hit new lows with 66% saying government is doing a poor job and only 18% saying it is doing a good one.

Historically, the relationship between this measure of consumer sentiment and actual consumer spending hasn’t been as good as with the Conference Board’s measure, but the sentiment reading surrounding the jobs market does have strong performance regarding labour market outcomes.

The chart below shows the relationship between fear of job loss and the actual change in private payrolls. It is a lead indicator presumably because workers see and feel changes in the workplace before they show up in the data – Workers tend to know if there is a hiring freeze or some other cost containment measures come in and go on to presume that jobs may soon be trimmed. That has historically proven to be the right assumption, so this does not bode well for the outlook.

U. of Michigan employment expectations versus the change in private payrolls (000s)

25bp October cut should be confirmed by a weak Beige Book

It also tallies with the jobs numbers we have been getting amidst the government shutdown. The ISM employment series is in contraction territory, while the ADP private payrolls report shows three outright falls in employment in the past four months. The Beige Book – the Fed’s anecdotal survey of the state of the US economy – is due out next week, and we are not expecting any meaningful improvement on its August messaging that 11 out of 12 Federal Reserve districts are seeing no net change, and the other is seeing falls in employment.

Fed Governor Chris Waller – one of the leading candidates to take over as Fed Chair next May – spoke earlier today, stating that “the labor market is weak and that’s the punchline for policy” and that the jobs surveys are “all telling you the same story”. He advocates a policy of ongoing 25bp cuts, saying “you can just do 25, keep going, see how it goes”.

We suspect a majority of Fed voters will end up agreeing with him and expect 25bp cuts at October’s and December’s FOMC meeting, with a further 50bp of cuts in early 2025.

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
